revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23984597483|manual triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:具注暦]]
{{Unreferenced|date=January 2020}} '''Guchū-reki''' (具注暦) refers to a {{ill|Calendar|en|Calendar|qid=Q12132|lt=Calendar|1=Calendar}} created and distributed by the [[Yin-Yang Bureau|Onmyōryō]] (Bureau of Onmyōdō) under the Japanese imperial court.
== Overview == The calendar was named "Guchū-reki" because various [[calendar annotations]] (暦注) for determining good or bad fortune were recorded in detail ("具" means "thoroughly"). It had a scroll format and was written exclusively in {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|qid=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}}. The structure typically included: - The top section listed basic elements such as the date, {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|en|Sexagenary cycle|qid=Q246658|lt=kanshi (干支)|1=Sexagenary cycle}}, [[:en:Na Yin|nattin (納音)]], [[Twelve Directives|junishiki (十二直)]], and other essential items or annotations. - The middle section recorded the [[:en:24 solar terms|nijūshi sekki (二十四節気)]] and [[72 pentads|shichijūni kō (七十二候)]]. - The bottom section contained additional annotations. Each scroll covered six months, meaning a full year required two scrolls.<ref group="note">Thus, one year comprised two volumes: upper and lower.</ref>
The oldest surviving Guchū-reki written on paper is the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|qid=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō|1=Tenpyō}} 18 (746) calendar from the [[Shōsōin documents]].
== Historical Use == During the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, the practice of using Guchū-reki as diaries began. By the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, the blank spaces on the calendar became increasingly larger for writing. A notable example is the diary of {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|en|Fujiwara no Michinaga|qid=Q433133|lt=Fujiwara no Michinaga|1=Fujiwara no Michinaga}}, which was written on a Guchū-reki and is known as the ''[[Mido Kanpaku-ki]]''. It is designated a [[:en:National Treasure of Japan|National Treasure]].<ref group="note">Such calendars used as diaries were referred to as "Rekki" (暦記) at the time.</ref>
After the late {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the rise of [[Kana Calendars|Kana-reki]] (仮名暦, simplified calendars written in kana) led to the decline of Guchū-reki's use. However, it remained valued as the official court calendar. By the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, its role had shifted from practicality to a "status symbol" for nobles and feudal lords.
The adoption of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|qid=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}} during the [[Meiji Calendar Reform]] marked the end of both Guchū-reki and Kana-reki.
== Notes == {{Note-help}} === Notes === <references group="note"/>
{{Japan-culture-stub}} Miscellaneous works ...Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/guchūreki|qq=}} and {{ill|Kana Calendars|lt=Kana Calendars|lt=Kanareki|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanareki|qq=}}, catered to specific audiences. January 2025 Calendar Science ...ons (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nakasei cale ... 5 KB (684 words) - 01:01, 18 January 2025
